ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'chicken tikka' class (FOODON_03302051). Pudina Hara Murgh Tikka is a mint-and-coriander variant; no exact FoodOn class exists for this specific preparation. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers grilled chicken meat. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for tandoor-grilled mint chicken tikka; closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific Indian chicken tikka preparation. Null is appropriate.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Pudina Hara Murgh Tikka is approximately 200 g, comprising 4 to 5 pieces of boneless tandoor-grilled chicken, served with mint chutney and sliced onion.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken tikka, grilled — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for grilled chicken breast (NDB 05013) for micronutrient detail
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) provides values for tandoori chicken / chicken tikka (grilled, marinated). Pudina Hara Murgh Tikka is a mint-and-coriander variant of standard chicken tikka; macros are consistent with the ICMR-NIN tandoori chicken entry (~165 kcal, ~25 g protein, ~6 g fat, ~2 g carbohydrate per 100 g). Micronutrient values supplemented from USDA SR Legacy grilled chicken breast where ICMR-NIN does not report the specific micronutrient. Cheese addition in some restaurant variants may slightly elevate fat and saturated fat; values reflect a typical preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for grilled/marinated chicken
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Pudina Hara Murgh Tikka. Chicken tikka and tandoori chicken are high-protein, very low-carbohydrate grilled meat dishes. The University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International GI Tables (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care) classify plain grilled/marinated chicken as GI approximately 0–35 (effectively low, often cited as ~30 for marinated grilled chicken). Carbohydrate content per 100 g is approximately 2.5 g, yielding a glycemic load per 200 g serving of approximately 1.5 g, which is negligible. GI of 30 (low) is used as a conservative analogous estimate consistent with approved GI source data for comparable grilled marinated chicken preparations.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from marinade spices, salt, and cheese
hypertension.guidance: A 200 g serving provides approximately 760 mg sodium (about 33% of the 2,300 mg daily limit), so individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-salt preparation when ordering from restaurants.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index (~30)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only ~2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a low GI of approximately 30, Pudina Hara Murgh Tikka has a negligible glycemic load (~1.5 per serving) and is a suitable high-protein, low-carbohydrate choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided sodium intake is monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein with moderate saturated fat; cooking method is grilling rather than frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tandoor grilling without added frying oil keeps saturated fat relatively low (~1.8 g per 100 g); the dish provides lean protein beneficial for cardiovascular health, though individuals with CVD should be mindful of the sodium content and choose preparations without added cheese to further reduce saturated fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pudina Hara Murgh Tikka is a North Indian tandoor-grilled chicken dish in which boneless chicken pieces are marinated in a vibrant green paste of fresh mint (pudina), fresh coriander (dhania), yoghurt, green chilli, ginger, garlic, and aromatic spices such as cumin and garam masala, then skewered and cooked at high heat in a clay tandoor oven. Some restaurant versions incorporate soft cheese (paneer or processed cheese) into the marinade, adding creaminess and a mild dairy richness. The result is succulent, slightly charred chicken with a bright herbal flavour, a subtle heat from green chilli, and the characteristic smoky aroma of tandoor cooking. The dish is closely related to Hariyali Murgh Tikka and is a green-marinade variant of the classic Murgh Tikka. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food (~25 g protein, ~6 g fat, ~2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, ~165 kcal per 100 g), making it suitable for high-protein dietary patterns. Its glycemic index is low (~30) and glycemic load per serving is negligible (~1.5), making it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is moderate (~380 mg per 100 g) due to the spiced marinade, which is relevant for hypertension management. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal. It is commonly served as a starter or appetiser in North Indian and Indian-Singaporean restaurants, accompanied by mint chutney, sliced onion, and lemon wedges.
common_user_queries: What is Pudina Hara Murgh Tikka?
Is Pudina Hara Murgh Tikka healthy?
How many calories in Hara Murgh Tikka?
Is mint chicken tikka good for diabetics?

How is green chicken tikka made?
What spices are in Hara Murgh Tikka?
Is Pudina Hara Murgh Tikka high in protein?

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for Pudina Hara Murgh Tikka exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA databases. Values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN tandoori chicken / chicken tikka entry (2017), which is the closest approved primary source for this preparation style. The mint-and-coriander marinade variant does not materially alter macronutrient composition relative to standard chicken tikka. Micronutrients are supplemented from USDA SR Legacy grilled chicken breast. GI is an analogous estimate from approved University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) peer-reviewed data for grilled marinated chicken.
notes: 1. Pudina Hara Murgh Tikka is a mint-and-coriander green-marinade variant of standard Murgh Tikka; it is closely related to Hariyali Murgh Tikka. 2. Some restaurant versions (as described in the field survey source) include cheese in the marinade, which may increase fat and saturated fat slightly above the values reported here; values reflect a typical preparation. 3. Sodium estimate (~380 mg/100 g) is based on typical tandoori chicken marinade salt levels from ICMR-NIN; actual restaurant sodium may vary. 4. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in approved sources. 5. FoodOn match is closest_parent (chicken tikka class); no exact FoodOn class for mint-variant chicken tikka exists. 6. FoodEx2 code A0FKP (grilled chicken meat) is the closest defensible anchor. 7. SNOMED CT: no applicable concept found; null is appropriate. 8. This dish is served at multiple North Indian restaurants in Singapore (field survey data: 5 restaurants). 9. Price range SGD 20.90–22.90 per portion (field survey data, 2024). 10.
